coredata.dc:title: Benefits of oxidation and size reduction of graphene/graphene oxide nanoparticles in biosensing application: Classification of graphene/graphene oxide nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Milosavljevic, Vedran
Mitrevska, Katerina
Adam, Vojtech
coredata.dc:description: Graphene nanoparticles (GNPs) have become increasingly attractive nanomaterials in their application in various biosensing platforms. Several benefits from the size reduction distinguish them from graphene (Gr) and graphene (GO), resulting from the electron confinement to smaller surfaces and increased edge-plane ratio. This allows for higher electrochemical activity due to the increased edge density and introduction of bandgap related photoluminescence even in GNPs that do not contain oxygen functional groups. The oxygenated counterparts, although less electrochemically active, are endowed with improved dispersibility and stability. Few aspects will be discussed in the presented review: a) the advantages and disadvantages of Gr and GO, regarding their electrical and optical properties; b) the properties of GNPs and their oxygen-containing analogs (GONPs) gained by the size reduction and quantum confinement effect; c) a clear distinction of GNPs/GONPs as nanoscale forms compared to the microscale Gr/GO; d) presenting a definition of GNPs and proper classification of the special forms of GNPs, graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs); e) summary of the proposed GNP biosensors will be provided, as classified into three main sections: GNPs, GNRs, and GQDs, with separate subsections for their oxygenated equivalents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zig-zag structure of (A) GNRs and (B) GONRs.
Arm-chair structure of (A) GNRs and (B) GONRs.

GONPs biosensors: A) Enzymatic biosensor for the detection of lactic acid, using LDH as a recognition probe and NAD+ reduction to NADH, resulting in current generation [78]. B) Enzymatic biosensor for the detection of oxalate, using oxalate oxidase as a recognition probe and H2O2 as a source for current generation [79].

GNRs biosensor mechanisms. (A) Immunosensor for the detection of ampyra, using magnetic immobilization of the Ab/Ag complex to the SPCE [89]. B) Enzymatic biosensor for the detection of glucose with glucose oxidase as recognition element [84]. C) DNA biosensor using stem-loop DNA recognition probe and Exo III activity to promote target hybridization [90].

GQDs fluorescent biosensors. A) Aptameric biosensor for the detection of acetamiprid using DNAzyme catalytic activity for the oxidation of OPD to DAP as FL quenching mechanism [97]. B) DNA biosensor for the detection of HIV-DNA, involving FL GQDs modified metal core and 3-cascade cycle amplification, to produce labeled DNA for FL quenching [98].

GQDs electrochemical biosensors: A) Biosensor for the detection of ractopamine, using aptamer as a biorecognition element, and AuNCs to increase the electroactive surface [108]. B) A sandwich aptameric biosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen combining hemin catalytic activity to provide current generation and N-graphene/AuNPs electrode modification to amplify the electrochemical signal [109]. C) Biosensor for the detection of miRNA, providing corresponding signal through intercalation of indicator molecule between miRNA-DNA duplex [110].

Fluorescent GOQDs biosensors. A) Fluorescence turn off biosensor for the detection of Pb2+ ions, using aptameric recognition and subsequent FL quenching [112]. B) Turn off/turn on biosensor for the detection of kanamycin based on competitive affinity of the aptamer towards kanamycin and SSB [114]. C) Turn off/turn biosensor for the detection of sulfamethazine based on FL quenching by GO and FL recovery upon detachment target/aptamer/GOQDs from the GO quencher [115]. D) Immunosensor for the detection of prostate-specific antigen, using sandwich immuno- biorecognition, silver core to quench fluorescence, and core disintegration for FL recovery [117].
GOQDs biosensor based on MIP biorecognition of a target and improvement of the electrochemical signal through GCE functionalization with NiS2, carbon source, GOQDs and AuNPs [120].
Electrochemical biosensors using GONPs as a sensing platform.

head.ce:title: Benefits of oxidation and size reduction of graphene/graphene oxide nanoparticles in biosensing application: Classification of graphene/graphene oxide nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene nanoparticles (GNPs) have become increasingly attractive nanomaterials in their application in various biosensing platforms. Several benefits from the size reduction distinguish them from graphene (Gr) and graphene (GO), resulting from the electron confinement to smaller surfaces and increased edge-plane ratio. This allows for higher electrochemical activity due to the increased edge density and introduction of bandgap related photoluminescence even in GNPs that do not contain oxygen functional groups. The oxygenated counterparts, although less electrochemically active, are endowed with improved dispersibility and stability. Few aspects will be discussed in the presented review: a) the advantages and disadvantages of Gr and GO, regarding their electrical and optical properties; b) the properties of GNPs and their oxygen-containing analogs (GONPs) gained by the size reduction and quantum confinement effect; c) a clear distinction of GNPs/GONPs as nanoscale forms compared to the microscale Gr/GO; d) presenting a definition of GNPs and proper classification of the special forms of GNPs, graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs); e) summary of the proposed GNP biosensors will be provided, as classified into three main sections: GNPs, GNRs, and GQDs, with separate subsections for their oxygenated equivalents.

ce:section.ce:para: The conception of biosensing analytical techniques originated in the early 1960 s, when a couple of enzymatic glucose biosensing platforms were proposed for the first time [1,2]. The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), defining the biosensor as a device that uses a biological component involved in a biochemical reaction that provides a quantitative or semiquantitative response, which can be further processed to a measurable signal, delivered the designated terminology. The three main elements comprising a biosensor are 1) a bioreceptor or a biorecognition molecule (enzyme, antibody, protein, nucleic acid, cells or tissues) directly recognizing and reacting with the analyte; 2) a transducer that processes the signal from biorecognition, converting it into a measurable signal; and 3) a display system that presents the transduced signal in a readable form [3,4]. The introduction of a biological compound is the main distinction between a sensor and a biosensor. The application of such biosensors is ubiquitous within a pool of fields, leading to medical analyses and pharmaceutical testing through agricultural and environmental safety [3,5]. The prospects of fast and accurate results, simple and easy-to-use devices, and the possibility for inexpensive multiplex detection in real time give biosensors the power to surpass time-consuming traditional detection methods, which involve tedious and complex procedures using massive laboratory equipment [6]. The added value of these biosensors is the possibility to miniaturize the detection system in a portable hand-held device, which would be suitable for in-field analyses, laboratories, and individual patient self-testing [7]. The advances in nanotechnology and microfluidic devices make it possible to construct such point-of-care (POC) devices.

The discovery and development of various nanomaterials makes nanotechnological progress evident, and the exceptional electrical and physical properties of such materials make them very attractive for biosensor fabrication. Nanomaterials provide a high surface area for biomolecule immobilization, and their ability to promote electron transfer and catalytic activities compensates for the lack of biomolecular electronic properties [4]. An exemplar of such nanomaterials for biosensing applications is the two-dimensional (2D) graphene (Gr) sheet with a planar structure of honeycomb-arranged sp2 carbon atoms. Its excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, high mechanical strength, and large surface area, as well as its biocompatibility and low toxicity, have increased interest in integrating such 2D Gr sheets in biosensor devices [8]. However, the lack of solubility in water makes the oxidized form of Gr, that is, graphene oxide (GO), preferable. GO maintains a hexagonal arrangement of carbons and, accompanied by the introduction of oxygen functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, epoxy and hydroxyl groups, switches to sp3 hybridization. On the other hand, this transition to oxidized form retards the electrochemical responses of this nanomaterial; thus, the sensor fabrication requires additional materials or compounds to enhance the electrochemical performance. In that regard, several sensors have taken advantage of bimetallic compounds involving transition metals. Namely, transition metal oxides and transition metal dichalcogenides have been employed in the fabrication of sensors for the detection of roxarsone and methyl paraoxon, respectively, using the synergistic effect of their combination with GO [9,10]. The benefits gained from the implementation of GO are a result of the presence of oxygen functionalities on a high surface area which allows for efficient electrocatalytic abilities, whereas the presence of the metallic compounds amplifies by improving the electrical conductivity of the sensor. In addition, this combination of bimetallic compound and GO effectively lowers the overpotential needed for the detection. Such biosensors have proven their practical application in meat and vegetables, as well as human samples such as urine and blood. On the other hand, the use or reduced GO (rGO) further improves the conductivity, while keeping the electrocatalytic ability, and the same synergistic effect with transition bimetallic oxides has been reported in a sensor for the detection of 3-Nitrotyrosine in human blood and urine [11]. The abilities for a fast charge transfer of rGO have been found beneficial in sensing platforms for gasses (NO2), by enhancing the detection capacity of transition metal oxides and transition metal dichalcogenides [12]. Furthermore, the nanocomposites of rGO and Cu2O or CsPbBr3 have a particular ability to produce photon induced current in a photoconductive switching manner, since the photogenerated electron-hole pairs have suppressed recombination, where rGO acts as an electron acceptor and as a channel for a fast electron transfer [13,14].

An improved performance of the graphenic materials, regarding their electronic and optical properties, has been also achieved through a size reduction, leading to the production of smaller Gr fragments that have been labeled as nanoparticles (NPs) [15], that is graphene nanoparticles (GNPs). The enhancement of the electronic properties of GNPs is a beneficial aspect of the quantum confinement, and additionally, the increase in the bandgap and the consequential photon-induced luminescence enables optical detection [16,17]. All GNPs retain their honeycomb crystalline structure; thus, they still adhere to the same Gr/GO standards regarding solubility and mechanical strength. Apart from the size change, GNPs can appear in various forms and morphologies, depending on the synthesis approach [18,19]. For instance, graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) take the form of narrow elongated Gr strips, which have extremely advanced electronic properties compared to regular-sized Gr, whereas graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the smallest Gr fragments (<20 nm) whose greatest advantage is the multicolor photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, which the Gr sheet does not provide [20]. Nevertheless, all GNPs gain a more pronounced properties due to the increase of the edge density and defects compared to the plane surface. This allows for richer edge chemistry than pristine Gr and introduction of more functional groups and more active sites, which all leads to enhanced electrocatalytic performance towards various targets [21,22].

Numerous biosensors have used GNPs as a biosensing platform due to their high surface area and biocompatibility for interaction with various biomolecules. However, these biosensors are still in a development phase that involves examinations of GNP-biomolecule interactions, the subsequent stability of such immobilization over time, and the stability of biomolecules in various environments and conditions [5]. Since GNP biosensor fabrication techniques are still at an initiation stage, the largest practical expansion of such biosensors has occurred in the last few years. Likewise, proof-of-concept publications have not provided a full and valid method for biosensors as portable devices. This limitation delays the possibility of producing such biosensors for commercial purposes. However, there are reports of some innovative GNP sensors that use screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) and universal serial bus (USB) connections between the device and a smart phone, providing a small hand-held display system [23], which can act as a stepping-stone towards biosensor commercialization. In addition, various sensors have already been proposed based on the improved electrocatalytic abilities of GNRs towards different target analytes, where the addition of metal particles additionally improves the electron transfer of such sensing system [24]. The have been applied directly to electrodes as GONRs and electrochemically reduced to improve the electrocatalytic properties and electrical conductivity for an overall improved sensitivity of the sensor [21]. Conversely GONRs were used as a composite together with multi wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) or bimetallic compounds (Bi2WO6) to take advantage of the synergistic effects of the composite resulting in increased surface for target interaction due to the presence of residual oxygen functionalities (electrostatic interactions) and aromatic domains (π-π interactions), as well as amplification of the electrochemical signal and minimized overpotential needed for the target in question [22,25,26].

In the past 10 years, multiple reviews have been published regarding various types of GNPs, some of them focusing on properties, structure, synthesis, whereas others establish the relationship between these data and their use in biosensing, sensing, therapy, diagnosis, and imaging. However, some discrepancies have been included in the identification of the nanomaterial as a GNP, originally presenting the use of GNPs, whereas later in the manuscript the same material is referred to as simple Gr nanosheet, which is a 2D microscale nanomaterial [27,28]. Various reviews cover the biosensing applications of GQDs, however a very common misconception is that the GQDs are not clearly distinguished from other types of carbon dots (CDs) and very often they are even mistaken for carbon nanodots (CNDs) and graphene nanodots (GrNDs) [29,30]. Cayuela et al., focused on presenting the fundamental structural and optical differences between GQDs, CQDs and CNDs, but despite the successful clarification, this classification is neglected repeatedly [31]. The reviews mostly focus on the photoluminescence ability of the GQDs and the electrochemical (EC) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) properties and their implementation in EC and ECL biosensing is rarely revised or the published data are old and need to be updated [32–35]. Additionally, the presence of oxygenated functional groups on these GNPs, is only mentioned in these reviews, pointing out that it affects the solubility [36]. It is true that various experimental articles present their GNPs products true to their oxidation level, entitling them as GNPs or GONPs, however, when they are summarized in reviews, these different nanoparticles are classified simply as GNPs, with no further subclassification regarding their oxidation status. Finally, there is a misinterpretation of the term biosensor, and various reviews cover sensors based on GNPs since all graphene-based nanomaterials have catalytic abilities. However, a biosensor true to its name needs to contain a biological molecule who will be included in the biorecognition event leading to a biochemical reaction, further amplified with the effect of the used nanomaterial, if such was used. Thus, in this review we will compensate for the deficiencies of the previous revisions and bring the information up to date. Based on the GNPs classification proposed by Talukdar et al., we will try to distinguish GNPs and the individual forms (GNRs and GQDs) as nanomaterials derived from a 2D Gr, which have retained the classic sp2 crystalline arrangement of the Gr structure, but each gained different electrical and optical properties based on their size and shape [18]. We will also include the biosensors based on GNPs that were not classified in either group (GNRs or GQDs) due to their irregular shape or lack of characterization of their shape, and therefore they were not revised before. The main categorization will be made based on the shape and size of the GNPs, depicting them as GNPs (general term), GNRs and GQDs, as well as their oxidation status (GONPs, GONRs and GOQDs). Additionally, we will include FL, EC and ECL biosensing approaches for all GNP classes and we will promote the proper use of the term “biosensor” by revising only the true biosensors which contain a biorecognition biomolecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene-based nanomaterials from the micrometer to nanometer scale and their effects on intrinsic properties

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) graphene (Gr) represents a single sheet of carbon atoms arranged hexagonally in a honeycomb-like crystalline lattice, reaching even micro- and millimeter in both lateral sizes [19]. This material is endowed with high intrinsic strength, thermal conductivity, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [28,37]; however, the structure based on sp2-hybridized C-atom interactions, connected through π-π bonds, imparts the most crucial benefits regarding its application in biosensing and imaging. The inherent cloud of delocalized electrons from Gr large surface area is responsible for the high charge mobility (electrons and holes) and thus the excellent electrical conductivity [38,39]. Additionally, this semimetal material has a zero bandgap, facilitating electron transport in which the electrons travel ballistically and act as if they have no mass as they move through the Gr sheet [37,40]. In terms of its interaction with light, Gr efficiently absorbs incident photons, creating electron/hole pairs that are separated when exposed to an external field, generating a photoinduced current [37]. Nevertheless, depending on the synthesis method, the structure and related properties vary. For instance, pristine Gr with minimal defects is obtained through mechanical exfoliation of a graphite source or through chemical vapor deposition (CVD), which is associated with excellent electrical conductivity (π-π bonds are intact), but the resulting solubility in water is restricted. On the other hand, methods involving strong oxidizing agents (e.g., the Hummers method) yield graphene oxide (GO), an insulating Gr variation with partially interrupted sp2 hybridization but great water solubility due to the presence of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups [41,42]. However, all these properties are dictated by the size of the nanosheet and can vary from the anisotropic electrical conductivity in graphite (where electrons mainly move through the sp2 carbon network, not between the individual Gr sheets in the stack) to the lack of a photoluminescence (PL) ability in a single Gr nanosheet due to the absence of a bandgap [38,43]. By selecting an appropriate synthesis method or adjusting an existing synthesis method, size manipulation of Gr can be achieved, which ultimately leads to the adoption of new features such as tunable band gaps and enhanced electrical properties. In fact, these enhanced properties are a result of the size reduction of Gr nanosheets to smaller fragments, with at least one dimension measuring between 1 and 100 nm, that are denoted as nanoparticles (NPs) [15].

Lateral sizes define graphene as 2D single sheet of sp2-hybridized carbon network extending to the scale of micrometer or millimeter in length and width. However, it has been documented that Gr can appear in nanoscale as well, where at least one side, or both sides, length and width reach approximately 100 nm [44]. In such cases, GNRs or GQDs are clearly described, where GNRs can extend to more than 1 µm in length and 50 nm in width, whereas GQDs reach 20 nm in both dimensions at most [18,19]. These two types have been identified as GNPs due to their smaller size than Gr, and the properties they obtain resulting from such size reduction. However, the synthesis of GNPs with diameters (length and width) of less than 500 nm has been reported, yet, they have not been given a proper definition according to their size [44]. Since they clearly differ from GQDs and GNRs regarding the size and shape, and at the same time cannot be identified simply as 2D Gr as they belong in the nanoscale group, an introduction of new terminology is long overdue. Thus, GNPs can be defined as nanoparticles with graphenic honeycomb crystalline lattice of sp2-hybridized carbon, which are restricted to lateral sizes (length and width) of approximately 100 nm and can appear as monolayers, few layers, or multilayer spherical structures ( Fig. 1.) [19].

The small nanoscale size and the corresponding large surface area can be achieved by adjusting the synthesis methods originally intended for Gr production. Namely, graphene nanoparticles (GNPs) can be generated though aqueous-phase exfoliation of graphite in a nonionic solution of a macromolecule (polyvinylpyrrolidone), facilitated by vigorous ultrasonication and subsequent centrifugation to remove the aggregated NPs [45]. On the other hand, the oxidized equivalents of such nanoparticles, graphene oxide nanoparticles (GONPs), are obtained through a modified Hummers method by subjecting graphite powder to the oxidizing effect of strong acids (NaNO3/H2SO4) for an extended time period of 24–48 h, followed by extensive ultrasonication to disperse the produced GONP aggregates into uniform single GONPs [46,47]. Alternatively, GONPs with more regular structures, fewer defects and retained aromatic profiles can be obtained with an improved method in which H3PO4 is used as an oxidizing agent instead of HNO3 [48,49]. The GONPs can be further reduced to reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles (rGONPs) by initially applying the Hummers method, followed by reduction with hydrazine [50]. Various enhanced properties are affiliated with the size reduction that occurs due to superior photothermal conversion, which is linearly related to the size minimization and ability to transfer the generated heat to other materials [45,50]. In this matter, GNPs outperform Gr and other carbonaceous nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes) despite their lower absorption in the near-infrared region (NIR) since they are more easily dispersible than Gr and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which tend to form aggregates in the solution and hinder photothermal performance [45,51]. Due to the large surface area and number of homogeneous adsorption sites, the size of the NPs affects the loading capacity of other compounds and allows such NPs to easily pass inside the cells and to be used either as drug delivery agents or for bioimaging [46,50,52,53]. GONPs are even more advantageous due to their oxygen-containing functional groups, which allow them to form hydrogen bonds or Lewis’s acid-base interactions with the –OH and –NH2 groups of other compounds, as well as π-π interactions with aromatic structures. However, GONP stability in a solution is pH dependent, as the optimal protonation/deprotonation degree maintains a negative surface charge that prevents aggregation. Aggregation is also a phenomenon associated with rGONP solutions due to the low availability of functional groups; however, due to their smaller energy gaps, rGONPs have the ability to absorb light at higher wavelengths than GONPs [50,54]. This aggregation is also evident in the differences between the electrogenerated chemiluminescence of GONPs and rGONPs. To prevent or minimize the aggregation of the reduced nanoparticles, surfactants, or modifiers such as poly-(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate), diazonium salts or ionic liquids could provide stabilization of the dispersed nanoparticles in water [55]. Alternatively, increase of the pH of the suspension can promote deprotonation of the reduced functional groups, and in a combination with ionic compounds like hydrazinium ions or potassium cations, they can counteract the Van der Waal interactions by introduction of repulsive electrostatic forces. However, the introduction of all these compounds may affect the conductivity and mechanical properties of the nanoparticles, thus a more appropriate solution can be provided by dissolving them in an organic solvent (e.g., DMF) with a proper polarity and matching surface energy to the surface energy of the dispersed NPs [55,56]. On the other hand, some GNPs are photoluminescent NPs that have the ability to emit light in different colors of the visible and NIR spectra, depending on their synthesis method [16]. Additionally, an upconversion PL (UPL) phenomenon is observed in these GNPs when they are excited with two or three lower-energy photons (with higher wavelengths of 700–1300 nm). Such excited GNPs emit light in the spectrum lower than ̴400 nm, which is highly convenient for maintaining a low degree of tissue damage with low energy excitation of the NPs during bioimaging [51,57].

The synthesis method is the main determinant of the morphologies, structures, and associated properties of the produced GNPs. Thus, diverse types of GNPs can be obtained, such as graphene nano-onions (GNOs), graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), graphene nanoplatelets (GNPls) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [18,58]. GNPls are disc-shaped stacks of multiple layers of Gr with diameters of 20–40 nm. The GNOs are hollow, concentric, multiwalled polyhedral structures in the shape of onions, with diameters ranging between 50 and 300 nm. GNRs are ribbon-shaped graphene sheets with widths of 20–90 nm and lengths up to 1500 nm, whereas GQDs most commonly appear circular or elliptical, although hexagonal and quadrilateral shapes can also be observed. Among these classes of GNPs, GNRs and GQDs are the most distinct, considering their structure and properties, and consequently, their applicability in various fields.

GNRs are ribbon-shaped, narrow Gr strips with nanosized widths of a few to 100 nm (usually < 50 nm), in contrast to their lengths up to 1.5 µm, and a graphenic sp2 hybridized carbon structure of the crystalline lattice. This unique shape endows GNRs with a high aspect ratio and increased surface area with an abundance of available straight edges [59,60]. Both bottom-up synthesis from carbon-containing precursors and numerous top-down synthesis approaches are available for creating GNRs; however, top-down synthesis is preferred due to the lower cost for a higher yield and production rate, despite the lack of control over the size and geometry of the NPs [61,62]. More precisely, methods involving longitudinal splitting or unzipping of CNTs through oxidative or reductive cutting, as well as electrochemical, sonication and lithographic methods, are used. The unzipping of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) generates few-layer GNRs, whereas single GNRs are obtained from single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). It is important to pay special attention to the size and quality of the CNTs, since the diameters of the CNTs dictate the resulting widths of the GNRs. Further, CNTs with small diameters (< 100 nm) are difficult to unzip, while CNTs with many defects tend to terminate the unzipping reaction on the defect points. Regardless of the synthesis method, size reduction induces the quantum confinement effect, giving rise to new electronic properties in the two main types of GNRs: armchair GNRs (AGNRs) and zigzag GNRs (ZGNRs) ( Fig. 2., Fig. 3.). The presence of AGNRs or ZGNRs can be confirmed depending on the angle of chirality for the C-atom edges, which further determines the performance of the structure. Namely, a zigzag orientation is associated with a localized nonbonding π state on the edges with high density, resulting in a metallic nature and increased reactivity due to the dangling bonds on the edges [59,63]. Such states are not present in AGNRs, and this electronic property leads to a lower reactivity and width dependence of the semiconducting or metallic nature for AGNRs with smaller or larger widths, respectively [64]. Similar to other graphene-based nanomaterials, the electrochemical activity of this material is at its highest peak on the edges and the fold lines of the wrinkled sites, rendering high electron transfer, which is also realized in the electrocatalytic performance of the peroxidase-like activity of GNRs [59,60]. The confinement of the carriers, along with the width measurement and edge configuration, facilitates bandgap tuning, supplementing the GNRs with a PL ability, enhancing the photocurrent and reducing the recombination of excitons through efficient charge separation. The absorption spectrum varies from terahertz to visible wavelengths, and fluorescence quenching is possible through electron transfer [59,62]. GNRs are susceptible to aggregation through π-π interactions; however, due to the oxygen-containing functional groups, GONRs are more easily dispersible in both polar organic and aqueous solvents [65]. This is attributed to the polar nature of the oxygen-containing functional groups, facilitating the dispersibility in solvents with appropriate dipole moment, including water and polar organic solvents such as dimethylformamide, N-methylpyrrolidone, tetrahydrofuran and ethylene glycol [66]. Additionally, the functional groups facilitate interactions with other compounds and biomolecules, either through the formation of hydrogen and covalent bonds or through π-π interactions and electrostatic forces [67,68].

GQDs are small, photoluminescent, single layer fragments of Gr, with diameters of less than 20 nm, and circular or elliptical shape, where the electrons are confined to a correspondingly small volume with a high surface area and aspect ratio ( Fig. 4) [18,38]. Similar to GNRs, GQDs can have zigzag or armchair configurations with exposed edges, enhancing their electronic properties [69]. However, the optical properties are the main feature that distinguishes GQDs from the other GNPs. The starting point that determines the optical properties of GQDs is the synthesis method. Although various methods, such as hydro/solvothermal and electrochemical cutting or even nano-lithographic and microwave methods, are in use, top-down exfoliation and acidic oxidative methods and bottom-up synthesis from polycyclic aromatic compounds, as well as polyphenylene dendritic precursors or fullerenes are most commonly used methods in practice [70,71]. The method of choice determines the shape, size, and degree of oxidation, which essentially control the PL. For instance, methods involving exfoliation in organic solvents or controlled bottom-up approaches produce pristine GQDs, whereas top-down methods using acidic oxidative agents (Hummer’s method) produce graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) with different degrees of oxidation [72]. Harsher conditions are applied in this top-down process (vigorous ultrasonication; increased temperature), and the longer the exposure is, the smaller the obtained particles are [73]. Ultimately, the size, shape and oxidation determine the bandgap size, which in turn governs the PL. Namely, the smaller size of the GQDs contributes to the increased bandgap due to quantum confinement, meaning that higher-energy photons have to be delivered for excitation, resulting in blue shifted emission towards blue or green PL [69]. In contrast, with increasing size, the bandgap decreases, leading to redshifted emission of GQDs from orange to red. According to shape measurements, the circular and elliptical morphologies are usually smaller than 17 nm with zigzag or armchair configurations, while only armchair edges are observed in polygonal GQDs with sizes larger than 17 nm. Higher GQD concentrations and lower temperatures stimulate aggregation of the GQDs, promoting redshifted emission. Additionally, the pH dependence of the GOQDs is caused by the protonation/deprotonation of the functional groups which induces aggregation in alkaline environments [72,74]. The degree of oxidation plays a significant role in PL; that is, pristine GQDs tend to emit in the NIR range, whereas the emission of highly oxidized GOQDs shifts towards lower wavelengths.

The PL properties of GQDs are efficiently incorporated in biosensing and bioimaging applications. The capability of functionalization with various biomolecules meets the fundamental requirement for biorecognition processes in biosensing, and additionally, the low toxicity, biocompatibility, and small size for facile penetration through cells and tissues enable the possibility of biosensing and drug delivery [69,75,76]. In addition, an excitation wavelength of 700–1350 nm is compatible with the biological window, and multiphoton excitation within this range causes UPL with anti-Stokes transitions towards lower-energy emissions. On the other hand, at higher concentrations (≥500 µg/mL), the GQDs induce secondary toxicity effects that are described as genetic and protein expression alterations, and also induce changes in the mitochondrial activity, affecting the transcriptomic and proteomic profiles [77].

GNPs, as a noncategorized morphological form of NPs (e.g., GQDs or GNRs), have been utilized in the fabrication of sensors only, which means that the biological component has been excluded. To the best of our knowledge, there have not been any publications reporting the use of GNPs in biosensing applications, although GNPs with defined morphologies have been exploited in sensor fabrication to a certain extent. However, it must be pointed out that the reason for this lack of GNP biosensor publications is that on various occasions, there has been inconsistency in the terminology within relevant publications; that is, oxidized GNPs have been presented as pristine structures, even though oxygen-containing functional groups are present to a certain degree, and vice versa. Nevertheless, since our focus has been directed towards the cooperative effects of graphene-based nanoparticles and organic components leading to a biological-response enhancement, i.e., construction of biosensors, we will consider biosensors that include GONPs as inorganic components in the detection platform ( Table 1). Namely, the few reported GONP biosensors exhibited electrochemical activity; thus, an electrochemical response was measured to determine the target molecule concentration in a sample. The selectivity of these biosensors has been tuned by the exploitation of enzymatic activity specified for a selected target, thus limiting false negative results in a complex matrix sample. A simple biosensor has been proposed based on the enzymatic activity of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) towards the reduction of lactic acid [78]. A pencil graphite electrode (PGE) was modified with GONPs, and further immobilization of LDH permitted many biorecognition sites for lactate molecules ( Fig. 5. A.). The oxidation of lactate into pyruvate in the presence of NAD+ leads to the regeneration of NADH molecules, which in return causes a change in the electrochemical response. The change in the current intensity is linearly related to the change in the concentration of lactic acid in the sample. Some enzymes catalyze reactions that result in the production of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which can be broken down into protons, oxygen and free electrons when subjected to certain potentials (Fig. 5. B.). These free electrons are responsible for the current flow; thus, any change in the H2O2 concentration results in a change in the current intensity. Such a biosensor has been developed for the determination of urinary and plasma oxalate [79]. A conjugation of polypirrole (PPy) and GONPs was used to modify the golden electrode (GE) and increase the current intensity, while the layers of polyaniline and chitosan enlarged the binding surface for oxalate oxidase, which was immobilized on the electrode through the linker molecule glutaraldehyde (GA). The oxidation of oxalate in the presence of oxalate oxidase produced H2O2, leading to a corresponding change in the current intensity (Fig. 5. B.). Similarly, Chauhan et al. used the PPy@GONP conjugate for electrodeposition on a fluorine tin oxide glass plate with an increased number of cyclic voltammetry (CV) cycles to create a thicker film on the electrode to maximize enzyme entrapment [80]. Again, GA was used as a cross-linking molecule to carry the bilirubin oxidase (BO) enzyme, which in the presence of bilirubin catalyzes its oxidation, releasing H2O2 and consequently changing the electrochemical response. An alternative for inducing the catalytic activity of enzymes has been presented by the preparation of enzyme nanoparticles (ENPs), which can provide substantially higher surface-to-volume ratios, resulting in more catalytic sites for the target molecule. A very straightforward mechanism using ENPs and GONPs has been developed for the detection of L-lysine in the serum of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-positive patients [81]. The PGE was modified with GONPs and lysine oxidase nanoparticles, which catalyze the oxidation of L-lysine into α-keto-ε-aminocarpoate, accompanied by the release of H2O2, which is responsible for the change in the electrochemical signal. On the other hand, Narwal and Pundir achieved excellent electrocatalytic abilities in a GONP-modified PGE by introducing two types of ENPs for the detection of glycerol in the serum of patients who suffer from hyperglyceridemia [82]. Initially, GONP-modified PGE demonstrated enhanced electrical conductivity due to the presence of GONPs; however, due to their isolating nature, the immobilization of glycerol kinase nanoparticles (GKNPs) and glycerol-3-phosphate oxidase nanoparticles (GPONPs) restricted the conductivity. More precisely, GKNPs and GPONPs as enzymatic nanoparticles require a specific substrate of which they will catalyze a specific reaction. In the absence of their specific substrate, glycerol for GKNPs and H2O2 for GPONPs, no catalytic reactions occur, and the electroactive products of such reactions are not produced. Thus, the GKNPs and GPONPs only physically occupy the surface of the electrode, hindering the electron transport provided by the current of applied voltage by the potentiostat. Nevertheless, when the appropriate substrate (glycerol) is present, GKNPs with an abundance of catalytic sites manages to convert numerous glycerol molecules to glycerol-3-phosphate by phosphorylation, The phosphorylated glycerol molecules are oxidized by GPONPs, resulting in the release of H2O2 and its subsequent electro-oxidation leads to current generation which is measured as a corresponding signal to the concentration of glycerol.

GNRs are elongated narrow strips of Gr with a high surface area and exposed edges, making them ideal for further functionalization with various nanomaterials and biomolecules ( Table 2). Metal NPs are a perfect fit for such functionalization; thus, Sandeep et al. used AgNPs as the primary material for modification of GNRs, and polyphenol oxidase was introduced in the composite for the detection of catechol [83]. This matrix was an ideal candidate for electrochemical detection since it prevented denaturation of the enzyme and its active sites, thus promoting the catalysis of catechol to quinone. Enzymatic reactions are the most reported biosensing mechanism that utilizes GNRs. Some attempts have been made to achieve glycose detection by enhancing the electrocatalytic performance, stability, and response permanence using metal oxides as GNR decorations. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) has been used for such purposes through further functionalization with glucose oxidase to catalyze the oxidation of glucose to H2O2 and subsequent measurement of the chronoamperometric response ( Fig. 6. B.) [84]. However, ascorbic acid and dopamine interfered with detection. The same group pursued another attempt at improving the biosensor by replacing MnO2 with bismuth (III) oxide, but despite this effort, the sensitivity was not improved and the interference from other compounds was not eliminated [85].

These researchers faced the same interference problem when using the same construction for the detection of ethanol with alcohol dehydrogenase, assigning this issue to the applied operating potential [86]. Moreover, they suggested that this biosensor is not suitable for human blood samples, although its use can be redirected towards the detection of ethanol in alcoholic beverages. Pajooheshpour et al. reported a bimetallic approach in which Au-Pt nanoclusters (NCs) capped with bovine serum albumin (BSA) were used to facilitate the surface modification of GNRs with functional materials. Namely, the synergistic effect of these two metals with GNRs improves the electronic properties and conductivity while enhancing the catalyzed oxidation of diazinon, producing a signal that can be measured by square-wave voltammetry (SWV) [87]. The synergistic effect between two metals, combined with the BSA template, was again utilized by this group in the detection of bisphenol A while implementing Au-Cu NCs in the composite, achieving excellent selectivity against other types of phenols [88]. On the other hand, a subset of the same authors applied the bimetallic GNRs approach without the BSA support and additionally modified this composite with uric acid (UA) as an indicator for oxidation current [89]. The Fe-Pt combination was carefully chosen to introduce the magnetic properties from Fe, while the Pt enhanced the conductivity and decreased the potential needed for the electrochemical reaction. Magnetic properties were needed to immobilize this composite to an SPE, avoiding the complexity of covalent bond formation or other types of immobilization interactions. Finally, the interaction between the UA and the target molecule, Ampyra, decreased the current, a response that was detected with differential pulse voltammetry. Such magnetic immobilization of a GNR-based composite has been applied in the fabrication of immunosensors. Hashemi et al. used magnetic GNRs combined with boronic acid (BA), which allowed the N-glycosylated Fc (fragment crystallizable) fragments of the antibody to interact with the BA, resulting in a perfectly oriented Fab (antibody binding fragment) region for binding the target antigen (Fig. 6. A.) [89]. Finally, GNRs have been modified with Au nanocages, which have an increased surface area and good conductivity due to the hollow interior and porous structure, allowing for increased loading capacity for stem-loop DNA molecules [90]. The stem-loop DNA hybridizes with the target DNA, which stochastically moves to the next stem-loop structure after the resulting duplex has been digested through exonuclease activity, achieving an amplified signal (Fig. 6. C.). The oxidation current is measured after the last hybridization of the undigested part of the stem-loop DNA, where complementary ferrocene-labeled DNA provides the electrochemical signal.

GONRs have a slightly inferior electronic properties compared to GNRs; however, their solubility in various solvents is significantly better, making them suitable for different types of samples. However, serving as a platform for numerous biosensors, GONRs are still capable of electrical conductivity and catalytic activities ( Table 3). Rodríguez et al. presented GONRs as a potential DNA sensing platform by homogeneous distribution of iron oxide NPs over the entire GONRs surface, which prevented agglomeration of the NPs and an increase in the surface area [91]. More precisely, these benefits allowed for an improved and more abundant immobilization of DNA molecules, and additionally, they detected a significant difference between the oxidation peak intensities of single-stranded and double-stranded DNA (ssDNA and dsDNA). DNA-based recognition was also applied with reduced GONRs (rGONRs). rGONRs were functionalized with AuNPs, which served as an anchoring surface for thiol-ssDNA and to increase the conductivity of the modified electrode [92]. The hybridization of a target DNA additionally decreased the current due to the negatively charged backbone, leading to electrostatic repulsion from the redox probe and subsequent changes in the CV and chronoamperometric response.

Regarding the optical biosensors, GONRs have been used as catalytic entities for the synthesis of optically active materials. Namely, Wen et al. managed to produce AuNPs through GONRs to provide catalytic reduction of HAuCl4 in the presence of H2O2, and the obtained AuNPs were able to induce surface-enhanced plasmon resonance (SERS) [93]. The immobilization of antibodies on the surface of the GONRs prevents them from executing catalytic functions; thus, it is not necessary to produce any AuNPs to provide the SERS signal. Once a complementary target antigen is present in the mix, it is recognized by the antibody, which in turn creates an antibody-antigen complex, releasing the GONRs to resume catalytic activity and to recover the SERS response. The same concept was adapted for the detection of lead ions (Pb2+) in water, with a simple introduction of an aptamer as a recognition biomolecule instead of the antibody [94]. To adjust the catalytic abilities of the GONRs, a controlled oxidation degree was maintained during the synthesis procedure of the GONRs.

The field of biosensing has just started to take advantage of these graphene-based nanomaterials; however, until now, GQDs are still in the lead as the most-utilized material in biosensor fabrication. An appreciable number of biosensors based on GQDs have been developed for various substrates of both organic and inorganic origins ( Table 4). In fact, the GQDs ability to interact with nucleic acids has proven useful for the detection of ions in different samples. In addition, doping GQDs with a heteroatom such as nitrogen improves their optical properties; thus, ssDNA has been proposed as a nitrogen source for doping since the fundamental components of DNA are nucleotides with nitrogen as an integral structural component. Given this knowledge, a fluorescent biosensor for the detection of Ag+ has been constructed by utilizing the interaction between GQDs and the long linear sequence of DNA, which increases the photoluminescence, followed by quenching due to the presence of Ag+ [95]. The high binding strength of the Ag+ nucleotides, has the greatest impact on quenching, and cytosine has the highest affinity to incorporate the ion in a cytosine-Ag-cytosine complex. Tetracycline has been detected in various animal-derived foods by a simpler fluorescent biosensor using cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes to quench the fluorescence of aptamer-modified N-GQDs and a turn-on mechanism to recover the fluorescence by complete detachment of the N-GQDs/Apt when it recognizes and binds the target molecule [96]. The fluorometric approach for the detection of acetamiprid provides dual amplification of the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme signal as well as the signals of GQDs functionalized with D-penicillamine and histidine [97]. Fundamentally, DNA contains a specific aptameric region for acetamiprid, whereas the rest of the DNA1 complementarily hybridizes with the middle section of DNA2, leaving its side guanine-rich sequences free. The formed triplex-to-G-quadriplex complex reacts with acetamiprid that has aptameric sequences, releasing DNA2, which self-hybridizes in the presence of K+ through its complementary guanine sequences, providing a binding area for hemin to create G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme ( Fig. 7. A.). This DNAzyme catalyzes the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) in the presence of H2O2. The catalytic activity of hemin is additionally improved by histidine modification of the GQDs, and such dual-amplified enzymatic activity leads to an even higher production of DAP, whose main role is to quench the fluorescence of GQDs, providing suitable information about the concentration of acetamiprid.

A GQDs biosensor accompanied by a three-cascade cycle amplification was designed for the FL detection of HIV-DNA (Fig. 7. B.) [98]. The interaction of pentaethylene-hexadiamine/GQDs (PEHA/GQDs) with rare earth metals has been used once again to improve the stability of the complex and to provide numerous amino groups for linker DNA (LDNA) immobilization and subsequent complementary DNA (cDNA) hybridization. The cyclic system is triggered by the target HIV-DNA sequence, leading to three-cascade cycles that produce multiple tetramethylrhodamine DNA strands (FsDNA). The hybridization of Fs DNA and cDNA brings the Fs DNA closer to the GQDs, resulting in FL quenching through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET). PEHA/GQDs have been additionally functionalized with histidine to design an FL biosensor for microRNA detection [99]. Double-cycle amplification triggered by microRNA is carried out to produce DNA sequences that hybridize with LDNA immobilized on PEHA/GQDs/HIS, and upon interaction with hemin, the hybridized GQDs complex becomes a catalytic unit for H2O2. As part of this complex, hemin quenches the fluorescence of GQDs, while its histidine-enhanced electrocatalytic activity towards H2O2 results in additional FL quenching.

A simple but innovative FL biosensor was introduced for the assessment of telomerase activity in the extracts of HeLa, A549 and MCF-7 cell lines [100]. Namely, the extracts of the cells containing telomerase are allowed to interact with a solution containing GQDs modified with telomerase sequence probes, primers, and nucleotides (dNTPs). Upon this interaction, drops of GO sheets are immediately added, which initially quenches the fluorescence of the GQDs. Meanwhile, the extension of the TS probe takes place directly on the GO sheets, creating sequences long enough to form G-quadruplexes when exposed to sodium chloride (NaCl). The G-quadruplex structures are unable to maintain the interaction with the GO surface; thus, they detach and consequently recover the FL.

A detection of CEA has been performed by a UCP fluorescence (FL) biosensor [101]. The upconversion enhancement was achieved by allowing hexadiamine/histidine (HAD/HIS)-functionalized GQDs to react with rare earth metals (Yb3+, Y3+, Er3+) through the abundant imidazole rings of HIS, resulting in a NaYF4:Yb, Er crystal covered with HAD/HIS/GQDs. The fluorescence of this complex was quenched through in response to Au-labeled hairpin DNA (Au-HP1) hybridizing to linker DNA immobilized on the photoluminescent complex through a carbodiimide reaction. The recognition process is conveyed through a mechanism involving HP2 with a CEA aptamer region, HP3 partially complementary to HP2 and exonuclease activity to produce signal DNA (SDNA). For each CEA in a sample, SDNA is released, hybridizing to HP1, and recovering the fluorescence by increasing the distance between the crystal complex and the quencher Au.

Finally, the p53 gene has been detected through a ratiometric FL strategy using N-GQDs and berberine as FL reporters [102]. The GQDs serve as a platform to immobilize a DNA sequence containing a berberine aptamer region and p53 gene complementary region. When berberine is added, it binds to the aptamer, and there are FL signals from both the GQDs and berberine, which can be distinguished by their specific emission wavelengths. In the presence of the p53 gene, the complementary sequence hybridizes with the target, and the introduced exonuclease cleaves only the nonhybridized DNA sequences, leaving the hybridized sequences untouched. As a result, the FL of berberine decreases linearly depending on the concentration of the p53 gene.

Apart from typical FL detection methods, the biosensors based on GQDs have adopted a number of other detection techniques using their optical properties. A DNA-ion interaction was applied to ions such as Hg2+, in an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection system [103]. The GQDs/DNA3 linked to AuNPs/DNA2 are used as an ECL nanoprobe, whereas a GCE is modified with a reduced graphene oxide/poly(5-formilindol) (rGO/P5Fin) nanocomposite to serve as an immobilization platform for DNA1. In the presence of Hg2+, the thymine nucleotides bind the ions through coordination chemistry, causing the DNA strands to align vertically, and with the addition of the ECL probe, DNA2 completes the sandwich complex thymine-Hg-thymine. Thus, the higher the concentration of Hg2+ in the sample, the more thymine-Hg-thymine complexes will form, and the more GQDs will be present to perform ECL detection. The target diversity of GQDs biosensors is undeniable, and in the context of diverse targets, we must ensure that nucleic acids, which have become extremely appealing detection targets for GQD-based biosensors, are not overlooked. Thus, a combination of an ECL DNA detection method and a multiple cycling amplification strategy has been proposed [104]. Cycling amplification involves multiple hybridization, elongation and nicking of the DNAs1 sequence triggered by the target DNA, resulting in an increased number of DNAs1 molecules. These DNAs1 molecules are recognized by the complementary region of Au/hairpin DNA (Au/HP) immobilized on GQD/polydialyldimethylammonium chloride/GO-modified GE, where the fluorescence is quenched by the close proximity of Au. The hybridization reaction, followed by the activity of the exonuclease, results in the cleavage of the Au-containing part of the HP, recovering GQDs fluorescence and enabling ECL detection. Wang et al. reported a resonance light scattering (RLS) biosensor for the detection of p53 mutant DNA, which uses two GQDs/HP probes in a catalytic hairpin assembly system [105]. Briefly, the target DNA unfolds and hybridizes with HP1 of the first probe, leaving a sticky end hanging for HP2 of the second probe to hybridize with and to replace the target DNA in the process. In this way, the target DNA cycles through many hybridizations, resulting in the creation of unique GQDs/HP1-GQDs/HP2 aggregates that enhance the RLS signal.

The quantum confinement is the most pronounced in the GQDs, since they have been resized to the smallest diameters, which provided additional improvement of the electrochemical abilities. The detection of chemotherapeutic agents such as topotecan has been attained using a carbon paste electrode containing GQDs, graphite powder and ionic liquid for electrode modification and a surface for double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) immobilization [106]. Topotecan can intercalate between the strands of dsDNA and to form a non-electroactive complex, which in return blocks electron transfer and decreases the electrochemical response. Electrochemical and fluorescent biosensors have been fabricated for the detection of other toxic compounds, such as acetamiprid insecticides. The electrochemical biosensor was designed with a unique star-like nanostructure composed of glutamic acid-functionalized GQDs (GluA-GQDs) and Au nanocrystals (AuNCs), where initially, the AuNCs are a result of Au3+ reduction with Glu-GQDs as a reducing agent in the presence of tannic acid [107]. The reasoning for choosing tannic acid instead of citric acid was the presence of many ortho-phenolic groups in tannic acid, which react with Ag3+, providing a foundation for nanocrystal interconnection and leading to increased electrical conductivity. Furthermore, the GCE was modified with a Gr aerogel whose 3D porous texture accelerated electron transfer, target recognition was conditioned by an aptamer, and the detection was carried out through differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements. An improved performance of GQDs in the electrochemical detection of ractopamine (RAC) has been achieved through functionalization with chitosan for better stability of GQDs in water and with cysteamine to provide thiol groups for binding with AuNCs [108]. In fact, the self-aggregation of AuNCs was prevented by introducing arginine, which simultaneously shaped the AuNCs into nanostructures with petals and tentacles that have higher electroactive surface areas ( Fig. 8. A.). The numerous aptamers immobilized on these AuNCs specifically recognize and interact with RAC, leading to hindered electron transfer and decreased current intensity. Hemin catalytic activity has also been introduced in the electrochemical detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), where it is assembled into a G-quadriplex structure connected to a CEA-specific aptamer that is used as a biorecognition probe [109]. Another aptamer is immobilized on the surface of the GQDs/AuNP coating of the N-graphene-modified GCE, and the presence of CEA causes the formation of a sandwich structure comprising the two aptamers and CEA in the middle (Fig. 8. B.). The detection is achieved through the enzymatic activity of hemin towards H2O2 which alters the current intensity measured by DPV. Finally, voltametric detection of microRNA was achieved by constructing a biosensor using glutaraldehyde functionalized GQDs on GCE as an ssDNA immobilization surface [110]. MicroRNA hybridizes to the complementary ssDNA, and the addition of p-biphenol results in its intercalation between the two strands of the DNA/microRNA duplex, which is recorded as a change in the DPV signal (Fig. 8. C.).

As fluorescent nanomaterials, GOQDs have been exploited in the fabrication of “turn on/turn off”-based biosensors ( Fig. 9, Table 5). Many ionic pollutants in water can be identified by biosensors using the “turn off” signals resulting from the quenching of fluorescence (FL) in the presence of such ions. The detection of Pb2+ has been achieved through FL quenching of the GOQDs when a water sample contains Pb2+ ions in a concentration range between 1 and 1000 nM (Fig. 9. A.) [112]. To introduce selectivity for Pb2+, the biosensor was combined with a microfluidic device that preconcentrated the target ions using cation exchange resins and simultaneously removed any anions present in the sample. However, the highest contribution to the selectivity was provided by the specifically designed aptamer, which recognized Pb2+ and bound to it upon contact while taking up the G-quadriplex structure itself. The close proximity of the ion leads to fluorescence quenching, thus enabling accurate detection of the pollutant. Another water pollutant, Cu2+, has been detected by aptamer modified GOQDs biosensors using the upconversion photoluminescence (UPL) abilities of these quantum dots [113]. In fact, the most important aspect of the UPL mechanism is that the excitation energy is lower than the emission energy, and this property has been exploited to avoid photodamage of the biomolecules (aptamers) when the GOQDs are exposed to a source of excitation energy. For this purpose, a femtosecond (fs) Ti:sapphire pulsed laser has been used as a lower-energy source with the added benefit that using this source eliminates errors resulting from accidental shorter-wavelength light from the monochromator. In contrast to these “turn off” biosensors, some antibiotics could be detected through biosensors by initial FL quenching and subsequent FL recovery in the presence of the target. GOQDs have been modified with a single-stranded DNA binding protein (SSB), and their fluorescence has been quenched by an aptamer binding the SSB [114]. When the target (kanamycin) was present in milk samples, the high affinity of the aptamer towards kanamycin resulted in detachment of the aptamer from the SSB while subsequently binding to the target, and consequently, the FL was recovered (Fig. 9. B). Alternatively, FL could be quenched by the adsorption of aptamer-modified GOQDs on a sheet of GO through hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking (Fig. 9. C). Sulfamethazine (SMZ) in milk has been detected by such a biosensor when the aptamer recognizes and binds to the target molecule, leading to detachment of the whole GOQDs@Apt/SMZ complex from GO [115]. The distance between the molecular probe and GO increased, thus disabling the quenching ability of GO. Apart from aptamers, ssDNA molecules have been used as complementary sequences for DNA detection, as well as carriers of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) acceptor molecules, enabling FL quenching. DABCYL-modified ssDNA has been used to induce electrostatic reactions with polyethyleneimine (PEI)-functionalized GOQDs through the negatively charged DNA backbone and positively charged PEI@GOQDs [116]. The proximity of DABCYL leads to immediate FL quenching; however, in the presence of the target DNA, a hybridization reaction between the ssDNA probe and target DNA occurred, and the dsDNA complex and DABCYL were separated from PEI@GOQDs, recovering the quenched fluorescence. GOQD-based biosensors have also been applied in immunosensing. The detection of a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) has been achieved through its double recognition where antibody-modified magnetic beads (MB/Ab1) act as a capture probe and GOQD@silver core nanocrystals act as a fluorescence probe [117]. Namely, the silver coating leads to fluorescence quenching of the GOQDs and provides an immobilization surface for a second antibody (Ab2). PSA is recognized by the MB/Ab1 capture probe, and upon the addition of the GOQDs@Ag/Ab2 complex, a sandwich structure is formed by placing a PSA between the two antibodies of the capture probe and the detection probe. To recover the FL of GOQDs, the silver core was etched by H2O2, releasing the GOQDs, and the magnetic beads were removed by applying a magnetic field (Fig. 9. D.). Enzymatic activities can be monitored through FL quenching of GOQDs, and such a method has been applied for recording NADPH activity [118]. More precisely, complex enzymatic reactions can provide insight into the activity of NADPH, since the oxidized form of NADPH (NADP+) can adsorb onto GOQDs and disable the peroxidase-like activity of GOQDs. An example of such an enzymatic system is the oxidation of dopamine into 4-(2-aminoethyl)benzene-1,2-quinone (AQ) and its subsequent restoration to dopamine through glutathione activity. In this system, the GOQDs catalyze the oxidation of dopamine, and the AQ absorbs onto the GOQDs surface, quenching its fluorescence in the process. In contrast, glutathione disulfide is reduced to glutathione, a reaction that is catalyzed by glutathione reductase, where NADPH is used as an electron donor, producing numerous NADP+ species. Glutathione reduces AQ back to dopamine, while NADP+ adsorbs on the GODQs, recovering the quenched fluorescence and simultaneously disabling the GOQDs from a secondary dopamine oxidation. In this way, the enzymatic conversion of NADPH into NADP+ can be quantified. Finally, the GOQDs can also be used as fluorescence quenchers. A convenient biosensing system has been developed for the detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in food samples [119]. GOQDs have been used as quenchers and immobilization platforms for FAM-labeled cDNA, which is complementary to one part of the aptamer designed for P. aeruginosa recognition. In the absence of P. aeruginosa, the aptamer hybridizes with cDNA, and its flanking region binds to the GOQDs, maintaining FAM quenching. However, the presence of P. aeruginosa in the sample triggers stronger binding behavior in the aptamer, and the aptamer binds to the bacteria with high affinity and detaches from the GOQDs, carrying away the hybridized FAM-cDNA. A larger distance between the FAM-labeled cDNA and the GOQDs was created, disabling the GOQDs from further FL quenching. The degree of FL recovery was a linear response of the bacterial concentration in the sample.

The optical properties of GOQDs have been exploited to a higher extent in the construction of biosensors; however, electrochemical biosensors using GOQDs as electrode-modification materials have emerged. The value of nitrogen-doped GOQDs (N-GOQDs) has been proven by demonstrating electroactive surface enhancement, and combined with AuNPs, N-GOQDs can be used to establish an improved electron transfer rate. An advantage of this combination is that it has been utilized in the fabrication of molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) biosensors for the simultaneous detection of dopamine and chlorpromazine [120]. In addition to the N-GOQDs, the electrode was first layered with NiS2 NPs to further increase conductivity, whereas the specificity of the biosensor towards dopamine and chlorpromazine was achieved by creating MIP-containing template cavities for both targets ( Fig. 10). The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) detection of one target did not impede the detection of the other target. Expanding beyond biosensors for a specific target molecule, cell-based electrochemical biosensors for toxicity evaluation have been proposed using cell-activity products as indicators for the toxic effect of the compound in question. For instance, xanthine and guanine concentrations in an MCF-7 cell solution have been attributed to the cytotoxic effect of fluorene against MCF-7 cells [121]. As the MCF-7 cell viability decreases due to the presence of fluorene, the concentrations of xanthine and guanine diminish as well, which can be recorded as oxidation decreases by the reduced GOQDs modified on a GCE. A more efficient approach for the toxicity evaluation of some pollutants has been developed by observing the change in the concentration of the cell-activity products, measured in a 96-well plate [122]. A good electrochemical platform with excellent electron transfer was designed based on the interaction between GOQDs and carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (GOQDs/CMWCNTs), which was used to modify a PGE that was subsequently dipped in wells containing human hepatoma (HepG2) cells previously exposed to the selected pollutants. As the pollutants have already caused some irreversible oxidative damage to DNA and changes in the cell’s metabolic mechanisms, the electrochemical signals from xanthine/guanine, hypoxanthine and adenine will demonstrate changes in their concentrations, indicating the degree of the pollutant’s cytotoxicity.

Advances in nanotechnology have brought about a new perspective for biosensing applications, presenting various nanomaterials that can be used to facilitate biosensor fabrication. These nanomaterials have many advantages, such as improved electronic and optical properties, and a high mechanical strength and thermal conductivity. As a biocompatible nanomaterial, Gr has a great advantage in biosensor manufacturing because it enables improved interactions with various biomolecules intended for the biorecognition of a wide range of target analytes in various types of samples. Additionally, its excellent electron-transfer and conductivity properties play a significant role in signal amplification; however, it lacks optical sensing abilities due to its lack of a bandgap. In contrast, the size reduction of Gr to sizes smaller than 100 nm permits the generation of a measurable bandgap. Such a bandgap introduces the possibility of new studies in optical biosensing based on newly produced graphene-derived NPs. As the size of these materials decreases, the prospects of increased electronic properties emerge, leading to the development and advancement of GNP-based biosensors with enhanced electrochemical responses.

The different types of GNPs dominate different branches of biosensing fabrication, namely, GNRs mostly occupy the field of electrochemical biosensors, while GQDs, with their prominent ability to produce PL within a wide color spectrum, are dominant in optical biosensor fabrication; however, both type of GNPs can be utilized in these two separate fields. The oxidized forms of these NPs known for their better solubility in various solvents; thus, their applicability is much more convenient, despite the slight decline in the resulting properties. The features of biocompatibility and low toxicity are still retained after size reduction; thus, biomolecule interactions are not compromised.

Despite the reasonable number of publications and biosensor proposals that involve GNP-based biosensors, practical applications have not yet been achieved. Before commercial implementation of these biosensors, some issues must be addressed. For instance, due to the limited evidence of GNP-biomolecule interactions, the stability of such interactions over longer periods cannot be assessed. Additionally, there is a need to examine the stability of biomolecules under various temperature, pH-level, and humidity conditions, and upon interaction with complex matrices, providing data about the interference of other compounds in the samples. Although some previous publications do provide such information, such data are either examined in laboratory-controlled conditions with spiked samples or the number of real samples is not sufficient to extrapolate an accurate estimate. All these issues delay progress towards validation of the functional biosensor concept, as well as cross-validation between different biosensors to assess the sensor-to-sensor variability. However, once this point of validation is reached, biosensors would be primed for use in a new phase of POC device development, including electrode design with the possibility of multiplex detection, as well as miniaturizing the whole equipment system, which would be the ultimate goal towards commercial success.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Vedran Milosavljevic defended his PhD in the field of Chemistry at Mendel University in Brno, CZ. He has published more than 70 research articles and/or reviews cited more than 600 times having h-index 14.
Katerina Mitrevska is PhD student of Biological Chemistry at Mendel University in Brno, CZ.

Vojtech Adam graduated in the field of Analytical Chemistry at Faculty of Science, Masaryk University, Brno, CZ. The PhD thesis was successfully defended in the field of Cellular and Molecular Biology at the same university. Now, he is the head of Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at Mendel University in Brno. He has published more than 600 research articles and/or reviews cited more than 15,500 times having h-index 58. He has been awarded by several national as well as international prizes including International Society for Electrochemistry Prize for Environmental Electrochemistry. In 2018, he was awarded by ERC Starting project.
